title: Integrating Artificial Intelligence into Citizens' Assemblies: Benefits, Concerns and Future Pathways
hypotheses: AI integration can enhance democratic quality and institutional capacity of citizens' assemblies while maintaining deliberative integrity
notes: Proposes analytical framework for evaluating AI benefits/limitations. Maps 11 ways AI/LLMs can be used across CA lifecycle. Uses Global Assembly case study and expert interviews. First detailed analysis of AI applications in CAs.
strengths: Comprehensive framework, empirical grounding, practical applicability, systematic analysis
weaknesses: Limited to single case study, focuses primarily on Western democratic contexts
citation: McKinney, S. (2025). Integrating Artificial Intelligence into Citizens' Assemblies: Benefits, Concerns and Future Pathways. Journal of Deliberative Democracy.

title: Computational ethics
authors: E Awad, S Levine, M Anderson, SL Anderson, V Conitzer, MJ Crockett, JAC Everett, T Evgeniou, A Gopnik, JC Jamison, TW Kim, SM Liao, MN Meyer, J Mikhail, K Opoku-Agyemang, JS Borg, J Schroeder, W Sinnott-Armstrong, M Slavkovik, JB Tenenbaum
hypotheses: Ethical challenges of AI can be addressed by incorporating study of human moral decision-making through computational reflective equilibrium
notes: Proposes computational ethics framework integrating philosophy, computer science, law, economics, and cognitive science. Introduces computational version of reflective equilibrium for engineering ethical AI systems.
strengths: Interdisciplinary approach, theoretical rigor, practical framework, cognitive science grounding
weaknesses: Theoretical framework needs empirical validation, implementation challenges unclear
citation: Awad, E., et al. (2022). Computational ethics. Trends in Cognitive Sciences, 26(5), 388-405.

title: The Question of AI and Democracy: Four Categories of AI Governance
hypotheses: AI governance can be systematically analyzed through four categories based on democratic input sources and binding authority
notes: Examines AI integration into democratic governance using Input-Process-Output model. Distinguishes democratic binding AI, undemocratic binding AI, democratic unbinding AI, and undemocratic unbinding AI.
strengths: Clear taxonomical framework, systematic analysis, addresses core democratic values
weaknesses: Limited empirical validation, focuses on categorization rather than implementation
citation: Han, S.J. (2025). The Question of AI and Democracy: Four Categories of AI Governance. Philosophy & Technology, 38, 83.

title: Critiquing the Reasons for Making Artificial Moral Agents
hypotheses: Arguments for creating Artificial Moral Agents are insufficient and potentially harmful
notes: Systematic critique of rationales for developing AMAs. Argues against moral de-skilling, responsibility gaps, and oversimplification of ethics. Influential paper in AMA debate.
strengths: Thorough critical analysis, identifies key risks, strong philosophical grounding
weaknesses: Potentially overly pessimistic, may underestimate benefits of careful AMA development
citation: van Wynsberghe, A., & Robbins, S. (2018). Critiquing the Reasons for Making Artificial Moral Agents. Science and Engineering Ethics, 25(3), 719-735.

title: Making Moral Machines: Why we need Artificial Moral Agents
hypotheses: We have strong reasons to continue responsible development of Artificial Moral Agents despite objections
notes: Comprehensive response to van Wynsberghe & Robbins. Argues for nuanced approach to AMA development based on context and purpose. Reframes debate from blanket opposition to context-specific evaluation.
strengths: Thorough response to critics, balanced approach, practical considerations
weaknesses: Theoretical analysis needs more empirical grounding, implementation challenges underexplored
citation: Formosa, P., & Ryan, M. (2024). Making Moral Machines: Why we need Artificial Moral Agents. PhilArchive.

title: Collective Superintelligence: Enabling Real-Time Conversational Deliberations among Humans and AI Agents at Unprecedented Scale
journal: Foundations and Frontiers in Decision Science
hypotheses: Conversational Swarm Intelligence can enable collective superintelligence through real-time deliberation at unprecedented scale
notes: Introduces Conversational Swarm Intelligence (CSI) combining swarm intelligence principles with large language models. Addresses limitations of traditional collective intelligence aggregation methods.
strengths: Novel technological approach, biological inspiration, addresses scalability challenges
weaknesses: Limited empirical validation, potential for manipulation or bias amplification
citation: Rosenberg, L.B. (2025). Collective Superintelligence: Enabling Real-Time Conversational Deliberations among Humans and AI Agents at Unprecedented Scale. Foundations and Frontiers in Decision Science.

title: Fostering Collective Intelligence in Human–AI Collaboration: Laying the Groundwork for COHUMAIN
authors: Pranav Gupta, Thuy Ngoc Nguyen, Cleotilde Gonzalez, Anita Williams Woolley
hypotheses: Human-AI collaboration can foster collective intelligence through complementary capabilities
notes: Explores conditions for effective human-AI collaboration in collective intelligence tasks. Identifies when humans and AI work best together vs. separately.
strengths: Empirical grounding, practical insights, comprehensive literature review
weaknesses: Limited to specific task types, generalizability unclear
citation: Gupta, P., Nguyen, T.N., Gonzalez, C., & Woolley, A.W. (2023). Fostering Collective Intelligence in Human–AI Collaboration. Topics in Cognitive Science, 17(2), 189-216.

title: Key challenges for the participatory governance of AI in public administration
authors: Janis Wong, Deborah Morgan, Vincent J. Straub, Youmna Hashem, Jonathan Bright
journal: Handbook of Governance and Data Science
hypotheses: Participatory governance approaches can address democratic deficits in AI deployment in public administration
notes: Identifies key challenges for democratic control of AI in government. Proposes participatory governance framework emphasizing deliberative practices.
strengths: Practical focus on public administration, democratic theory grounding, policy relevance
weaknesses: Limited empirical validation, implementation barriers underexplored
citation: Wong, J., et al. (2022). Key challenges for the participatory governance of AI in public administration. In Handbook of Governance and Data Science. Edward Elgar Publishing.

title: Innovative Citizen Participation and New Democratic Institutions: Catching the Deliberative Wave
hypotheses: Representative deliberative processes can address complex policy problems and strengthen democratic governance
notes: Comprehensive analysis of 300+ representative deliberative practices worldwide. Maps trends, models, and trade-offs in citizens' assemblies, juries, and panels.
strengths: Extensive empirical base, international scope, practical guidance for practitioners
weaknesses: Descriptive rather than evaluative, limited theoretical framework
citation: OECD (2020). Innovative Citizen Participation and New Democratic Institutions: Catching the Deliberative Wave. OECD Publishing.

title: Evaluating the role of citizens' juries in urban climate governance
hypotheses: Citizens' juries can enhance climate governance while addressing procedural and representation justice concerns
notes: Case study of Leeds citizens' climate jury. Examines procedural justice, representation justice, and power differentials in deliberative processes.
strengths: Empirical case study, justice framework, practical insights for facilitators
weaknesses: Single case study, limited generalizability, context-specific findings
citation: Middlemiss, L. (2021). Evaluating the role of citizens' juries in urban climate governance. Local Environment, 26(12), 1588-1604.

title: Institutionalizing Democratic Innovations in Poland: Mapping the Evolution of Citizens' Assemblies Through Rules of Procedure
hypotheses: Citizens' assemblies can be successfully institutionalized through systematic evolution of rules of procedure
notes: Analysis of CA institutionalization in Poland, first CEE country to join deliberative wave. Uses neo-institutionalism to examine Rules of Procedure evolution.
strengths: Longitudinal analysis, institutionalism framework, CEE context valuable
weaknesses: Limited to single country context, focus on formal rules vs. practice
citation: Pospieszna, P., & Hoffmann, M. (2025). Institutionalizing Democratic Innovations in Poland. Politics and Governance, 14.

title: Deliberative democracy as a tool to foster youth participation in Europe: the case of Citizens' Juries in Sierra Norte
hypotheses: Citizens' juries can address declining youth electoral participation through enhanced civic engagement
notes: Examines use of citizens' juries to address youth political participation crisis. Case study from Sierra Norte region focusing on deliberative democracy as solution.
strengths: Addresses important demographic challenge, empirical case study, policy relevance
weaknesses: Limited to youth participation, single case study, generalizability questions
citation: Soto-Sainz, Ó. (2025). Deliberative democracy as a tool to foster youth participation in Europe. Frontiers in Political Science, 3.

title: Citizens' juries in planning research priorities: process, engagement and outcome
authors: Rachael Gooberman‐Hill, Jeremy Horwood, Michael Calnan
hypotheses: Citizens' juries can effectively engage public in health research priority setting
notes: Case study of Bristol Citizens' Jury for health research priorities. 20 jurors, 11 sessions over 16 weeks. Examines engagement, representation, and deliberation processes.
strengths: Detailed process analysis, long-term engagement study, practical insights
weaknesses: Single case study, dated research, limited comparative analysis
citation: Gooberman‐Hill, R., Horwood, J., & Calnan, M. (2006). Citizens' juries in planning research priorities. Health Expectations, 9(4), 359-372.

title: Variations of Institutional Design for Empowered Deliberation
hypotheses: Empowered deliberation requires careful institutional design balancing deliberative quality with formal authority
notes: Analyzes formally empowered deliberative processes with credible formal authority. Identifies four key design dimensions and trade-offs in institutional design.
strengths: Conceptual clarity, institutional design focus, comparative analysis
weaknesses: Limited empirical validation, theoretical framework needs testing
citation: Johnson, C., & Gastil, J. Variations of Institutional Design for Empowered Deliberation. Journal of Public Deliberation.

title: Artificial Intelligence, Participatory Democracy, and Responsive Government
hypotheses: AI poses significant risks to participatory democracy through potential manipulation of public comment processes
notes: Examines risks of AI manipulation in public comment systems, using FCC net neutrality case. Proposes governance responses to protect democratic participation.
strengths: Practical focus on current risks, policy recommendations, real-world examples
weaknesses: Focus on risks rather than opportunities, limited scope to comment systems
citation: Panditharatne, M. (2023). Artificial Intelligence, Participatory Democracy, and Responsive Government. Brennan Center for Justice.

title: Participatory approaches should be used to address the ethics of social media experiments
authors: Vincent J Straub, Jason W Burton
hypotheses: Participatory governance approaches are essential for addressing ethical challenges in large-scale AI/social media experimentation
notes: Argues for participatory approaches to govern ethics of social media experiments. Connects to broader questions of AI governance and public participation.
strengths: Connects ethics to participation, practical governance focus, timely topic
weaknesses: Limited to social media context, theoretical rather than empirical
citation: Straub, V.J., & Burton, J.W. (2025). Participatory approaches should be used to address the ethics of social media experiments. Communications Psychology, 3, 28.

title: AIMED: Towards a Philosophically Legitimated AI-assisted Iterative Method for Ethical Deliberation
hypotheses: AI assistance in ethical deliberation can be legitimate through proper philosophical grounding and human oversight
notes: Proposes AIMED framework with five legitimacy criteria for AI-assisted ethical deliberation. Addresses philosophical foundations and regulatory alignment.
strengths: Philosophical rigor, practical framework, regulatory alignment, legitimacy criteria
weaknesses: Theoretical proposal needs empirical validation, implementation challenges unclear
citation: Rivelli, L. (2025). AIMED: Towards a Philosophically Legitimated AI-assisted Iterative Method for Ethical Deliberation. PhilSci Archive.

title: AI-enhanced collective intelligence
hypotheses: AI can enhance human collective intelligence through complementary capabilities in multilayer network systems
notes: Proposes multilayer network representation of human-AI collective intelligence with cognition, physical, and information layers. Examines diversity and interaction effects.
strengths: Network science approach, systematic framework, interdisciplinary perspective
weaknesses: Theoretical model needs empirical validation, complexity may limit practical application
citation: Cui, H., & Yasseri, T. (2024). AI-enhanced collective intelligence. Patterns, 5(9).

title: Generative AI and the Future of Democratic Citizenship
authors: Paul Formosa, Bhanuraj Kashyap, Siavosh Sahebi
hypotheses: Generative AI technologies will have transformative impacts on democratic citizenship and deliberative processes
notes: Explores potential impacts of Generative AI on democracy and citizenship. Draws on deliberative democracy theory and reciprocal impacts with social media.
strengths: Forward-looking analysis, deliberative democracy grounding, comprehensive scope
weaknesses: Speculative analysis, limited empirical grounding, implementation unclear
citation: Formosa, P., Kashyap, B., & Sahebi, S. (2025). Generative AI and the Future of Democratic Citizenship. AI Magazine.

title: When humans and AI work best together — and when each is better alone
authors: Thomas W. Malone, Abdullah Almaatouq, Michelle Vaccaro
hypotheses: AI-human combinations do not automatically outperform best individual systems; success depends on task characteristics
notes: Meta-analysis of 100+ studies on human-AI collaboration. Surprising finding that combinations often underperform. Identifies conditions for successful collaboration.
strengths: Large-scale meta-analysis, counterintuitive findings, practical insights
weaknesses: May discourage collaboration research, findings may be task-specific
citation: Malone, T.W., Almaatouq, A., & Vaccaro, M. (2024). When do humans and AI work best together? Nature Human Behaviour.
